warframe-market.oneLiner: The de-facto community marketplace where Warframe players post buy/sell orders for nearly every tradeable item and price-check before trading.
warframe-market.overview: Warframe Market is a fan-created (not officially operated by Digital Extremes) web platform that acts as a centralized order board for player-to-player trading in Warframe. Players list buy and sell orders for prime parts/sets, mods, relics, blueprints, arcanes, Rivens (via an auction system), Kuva Liches/Sisters, and Captura scenes, with separate marketplaces for PC, PlayStation, Xbox and Switch. Trades themselves still happen in-game; the site copies a pre-formatted whisper to your clipboard to paste into trade chat. It also exposes a public API (rate-limited, Cloudflare-protected) that powers a large ecosystem of third-party tools and extensions.
keyFeatures: Buy/sell order board covering nearly every tradeable item (sets, parts, mods, relics, arcanes, blueprints)
Dedicated Riven mod auction system with bidding
Live online-status filtering so you see orders from players currently in-game
Auto-generated in-game whisper copied to clipboard for the actual trade
Price statistics / recent-order history for price checking
Separate platform marketplaces (PC, PlayStation, Xbox, Switch)
Public rate-limited API (3 req/s) enabling third-party tools, bots and extensions
Kuva Lich / Sisters of Parvos and Captura scene trading
warframe-market.bestFor: Any Warframe player who wants to buy or sell items for platinum efficiently, price-check before trading, or sell/buy Riven mods via auction.
warframe-market.sentimentSummary: Warframe Market is overwhelmingly treated by the community and content creators as the default, essential trading tool — routinely recommended as the most accurate and practical way to trade for platinum outside in-game chat. Common criticisms are structural rather than about the site's reliability: listed "average" prices can be stale or skewed, buyers frequently lowball off those numbers, and the site only lists orders rather than executing trades, so you still deal with unresponsive or AFK traders. Its API strength has also spawned many companion tools that layer analytics on top.

safety.notes: Web-based, read-only order board. It does not read or inject into game memory and does not require your Warframe/DE account credentials (it uses its own site account), so it falls outside DE's concerns about intrusive overlays. Actual trades occur in-game via manual whisper, and no real-money transactions are involved (distinguishing it sharply from RMT sites like Odealo). Widely tolerated and de-facto endorsed across the community. Only general trading caution applies: verify prices and be wary of the usual scam patterns during the in-game trade itself.

warframe-market.tierReason: It is the single most widely used and recommended Warframe trading resource, forms the backbone of the third-party tool ecosystem via its API, and is treated as a must-have by both new and veteran players.
pros: The de-facto standard marketplace with by far the largest pool of active orders
Covers essentially every tradeable item type, including a dedicated Riven auction system
Free to use with no real-money transactions
Online-status filtering surfaces traders you can reach right now
Auto-generated whisper makes contacting a trader fast
Open, documented API powers a rich ecosystem of companion tools and extensions
cons: Only lists orders — it does not execute trades, so you still coordinate manually in-game
Displayed average prices can be stale or skewed, and buyers often lowball based on them
Unresponsive, AFK, or already-sold listings are common friction points
Fan-made and unaffiliated with Digital Extremes (no official support)
API is Cloudflare-protected and rate-limited (3 req/s), which can block heavier tooling
Some players report the site whisper/in-game chat linkage not always working

warframe-wiki.oneLiner: The official, community-run WARFRAME encyclopedia, now self-hosted at wiki.warframe.com after leaving Fandom with Digital Extremes' backing.
warframe-wiki.overview: WARFRAME Wiki is the comprehensive, community-maintained knowledge base for Digital Extremes' free-to-play game Warframe, covering warframes, weapons, companions, enemies, mods, drop tables, quests, lore, and game mechanics. In 2025 the wiki migrated off Fandom to its own domain (wiki.warframe.com); Digital Extremes officially endorses this new site, covers its hosting costs, and provides SEO help and assets, while Weird Gloop (the organization behind the RuneScape, OSRS, and Minecraft wikis) assists with backend support. The move was driven by long-standing community frustration with Fandom's heavy ads, auto-playing videos, pop-ups, and poor performance. The new site carries over past content and comment history and is where all future updates are documented.
keyFeatures: Detailed pages for warframes, weapons, companions, and enemies with stats
Drop tables, mission rewards, and resource-location data
Modding, damage, and progression mechanics guides
Quest walkthroughs, codex entries, and lore documentation
Live open-world cycle clocks (Cetus/Plains, Orb Vallis, etc.)
Patch notes, event coverage, and Prime Resurgence info
Ad-free, high-performance self-hosted MediaWiki (desktop and mobile)
Comment sections and community editing
warframe-wiki.bestFor: Any Warframe player, new or veteran, who needs authoritative game data: build stats, drop sources, quest steps, or mechanics details.
warframe-wiki.sentimentSummary: Community sentiment toward the migration is strongly positive: players had complained for years about Fandom's intrusive ads and sluggish, sometimes unreadable pages, and the move to a fast, ad-free, DE-endorsed site was widely welcomed. The wiki itself is regarded as the single most essential reference for the game. The main lingering points are transitional confusion (outdated Fandom pages still ranking in search) and the usual caveat that community-edited data can occasionally lag behind patches.

safety.notes: Read-only web resource with no game-memory access, no login to your Warframe account, and no client-side software. Officially endorsed by Digital Extremes, who fund the hosting. No ToS, overlay, or RMT concerns. Note the legacy Fandom wiki (warframe.fandom.com) is no longer the official/maintained version and may show stale info.

warframe-wiki.tierReason: The definitive, officially endorsed reference for Warframe, used broadly across the entire player base; ad-free self-hosting and DE backing make it the default first stop for game information.

overframe.oneLiner: A large community-driven Warframe build database and builder where players share, vote on, and browse loadouts for every frame, weapon, and companion.
overframe.overview: Overframe (overframe.gg) is a web-based Warframe build platform operated by M.O.B.A. Network AB (part of the MagicFind/MOBA gaming network). It lets players use an advanced mod/build editor to create, share, and browse community loadouts, and it aggregates upvotes/downvotes into tier lists and "top build" rankings for warframes, weapons, and companions. The site advertises a very large content base (on the order of millions of submitted builds and tens of millions of votes). A companion desktop app is also distributed via Overwolf (an Electron-based overlay with build editing and in-game Player Sync of your arsenal).
keyFeatures: Advanced browser-based build/mod editor for warframes, weapons, and companions
Community-submitted builds with upvote/downvote ranking
Auto-generated tier lists and 'best builds' rankings
Build guides and equipment/mod reference data
Search and browse across a very large build library
Optional Overwolf desktop app with in-game overlay and Player Sync (auto-imports your arsenal)
Free to use (ad-supported)
overframe.bestFor: Players who want a quick starting-point build for any frame or weapon and like browsing many community variations, rather than experts seeking guaranteed-optimal meta builds.
overframe.sentimentSummary: Overframe is widely known and heavily used as a build reference, but community sentiment is genuinely mixed. Fans value the huge library, easy builder, and tier lists as a fast way to get a workable loadout. Critics repeatedly warn that vote-based ranking surfaces low-quality, outdated, or gimmicky builds, that popular creators can inflate their own builds' rankings, and that builds should be treated as inspiration rather than gospel. A common refrain is 'good as a starting point, don't copy blindly.'

safety.notes: The core product is a read/write website with no game-memory access and no account credentials required to browse. The optional companion runs on Overwolf, a DE-sanctioned overlay framework that does not read Warframe's game memory (unlike injection-based cheats); its Player Sync imports your arsenal via Warframe's public profile data, not memory reads. No RMT. Main practical risk is build quality, not account safety. The desktop app is ad-supported.

overframe.tierReason: One of the largest and most recognized Warframe build resources with broad adoption and a capable free builder, but placed at 'recommended' rather than 'essential' because build quality is inconsistent and community trust in its rankings is only moderate.
pros: Massive library of community builds for nearly every item
Free, capable web build editor with no login required to browse
Tier lists and rankings give fast direction for new/returning players
Optional Overwolf app syncs your actual in-game arsenal
Long-established, well-known site backed by a professional gaming network
cons: Vote-based ranking often surfaces suboptimal, outdated, or gimmick builds
Popular creators' builds can be over-ranked regardless of quality
Builds frequently lag behind balance changes/updates
Requires personal judgment; copying top builds blindly is discouraged by the community
Ad-supported (desktop app in particular)

warframestat-api.oneLiner: The community-run REST/WebSocket API that parses Warframe's official worldState into clean JSON, powering most third-party trackers and Discord bots.
warframestat-api.overview: The Warframe Status API (warframestat.us), maintained by the Warframe Community Developers (WFCD), is an open-source service that parses Warframe's official worldState.php feed and static game data into an accessible, well-documented JSON API. It exposes real-time worldstate (fissures, alerts, invasions, sorties, Nightwave, Cetus/Vallis cycles, Baro, void trader, events) plus static reference data (items, weapons, mods, warframes, drop tables, riven data) across PC, PlayStation, Xbox and Switch, with both REST endpoints and a WebSocket feed. It is the de-facto data backbone for a large share of the Warframe tool ecosystem, including Warframe Hub, the Cephalon Genesis Discord bot, and countless community projects.
keyFeatures: Real-time worldstate: fissures, alerts, invasions, sorties, Nightwave, Baro/void trader, day-night cycles, events
Static game data: items, weapons, warframes, mods, drop tables, riven data
REST endpoints (api.warframestat.us/{platform}/{lang}) plus a WebSocket feed for live updates
Multi-platform: PC, PlayStation, Xbox, Nintendo Switch
Multi-language localization support
OpenAPI 3 spec and hosted docs at docs.warframestat.us
Fully open source (Apache-2.0), NestJS/TypeScript, ~357 unit tests, 80%+ coverage
No API key or account required; free public access
warframestat-api.bestFor: Developers and tool/bot builders who need reliable, structured Warframe worldstate and item data without parsing DE's raw worldState.php themselves.
warframestat-api.sentimentSummary: Among Warframe developers the API is treated as the trusted, standard data source, cited on the official forums and reused by many community projects, bots, and sites like Warframe Hub. It is open source, actively maintained (400+ releases, latest in the 3.2.x line), and funded via a community Patreon for hosting. Direct end-user reviews are scarce because it is infrastructure rather than a consumer app; the main practical caveats developers note are occasional data lag or gaps when DE changes the underlying worldState format.

safety.notes: Read-only public web API with no login, no account, and no game-memory access, so it carries no ToS or account risk. Fully open source under Apache-2.0, maintained by the well-established Warframe Community Developers (WFCD). Reputation-check sites flag the domain as clean (no malware/phishing). It does not read game memory or overlay the client, so DE's overlay/anti-cheat concerns do not apply.

warframestat-api.tierReason: For developers it is effectively the de-facto standard worldstate API and underpins much of the community tool ecosystem, but its audience is builders rather than everyday players, so it lands at 'recommended' rather than 'essential' on a general tool vault.
pros: Free, open source (Apache-2.0), no API key required
Comprehensive worldstate plus static item/mod/weapon/drop data
REST and WebSocket access with an OpenAPI spec and hosted docs
Multi-platform and multi-language
Actively maintained by WFCD with frequent releases and strong test coverage
Powers Warframe Hub, Cephalon Genesis, and many community tools, signaling proven reliability
cons: Aimed at developers, not directly usable by average players
Depends on DE's worldState feed, so data can lag or break when DE changes formats
Hosting is community-funded via Patreon, so uptime relies on volunteer resources
No official SLA or guaranteed rate/availability
Little consumer-facing review presence to gauge sentiment

otherReviews.snippet: Open-source NestJS app that parses worldState.php; Apache-2.0 licensed, 400+ releases, ~357 unit tests with 80%+ coverage, actively maintained by the Warframe Community Developers.
Community thread referencing warframestat.us and WFCD tooling as the go-to worldstate data resource for building trackers and bots.
The API is open source and feeds data for Cephalon Genesis, Warframe Hub, and numerous other community-maintained tools; supported by a public Discord and a Patreon covering hosting costs.

wfinfo.oneLiner: A free, open-source OCR overlay that instantly shows the Platinum and Ducat value of every item on a Warframe relic reward screen so you can pick the best drop.
wfinfo.overview: WFInfo is a Windows companion app (by the Warframe Community Developers / WFCD) that screenshots the fissure reward screen, runs the cropped item text through Google's Tesseract OCR, and overlays each reward's warframe.market Platinum price, Ducat value, sold volume, vaulted status, and how many you own. It runs either on a hotkey or in an "Auto" mode that watches the game's EE.log for the reward event. It is screenshot-based and does not read game memory. A Linux community port, wfinfo-ng, also exists.
keyFeatures: OCR reading of the relic reward screen (Tesseract) with an overlay or side window
Live Platinum and Ducat prices, sold volume, and vaulted status pulled from warframe.market
Auto mode that detects the reward screen via EE.log, plus configurable hotkey activation
Shows owned/mastered count so you can avoid duplicate rewards
Snap-it inventory scanning and Master-it bulk mastery marking
Relic and equipment info panels; clipboard export of prices to chat
warframe.market connectivity for managing listings
wfinfo.bestFor: Relic/fissure farmers and traders who want to instantly know which of the four reward choices is worth the most Platinum or Ducats without alt-tabbing to a price site.
wfinfo.sentimentSummary: WFInfo is one of the longest-standing and most-recommended Warframe farming utilities, widely cited as the go-to relic reward price checker and frequently paired with tools like AlecaFrame in community guides. Its most debated point is safety, but the community consensus (backed by a past DE staff forum statement) is that its screenshot-only approach is allowed. Common complaints are practical: OCR occasionally misreads items and it requires specific display/UI settings (Borderless, Item Labels on) to work reliably.

safety.notes: Open-source (Apache-2.0) and screenshot/OCR-based only — it does not read or modify game memory, which is the key distinction DE draws for overlay legality. A DE developer (Aidan, now retired) stated on the official forums that using WFInfo would not result in a ban, and it is broadly treated as safe by the community. Rated 'caution' rather than 'safe' only because it is an external overlay that hooks the game window and reads the debug log; it is not endorsed/official and could in principle change status, so use the official WFCD source (GitHub/Nexus) and avoid unofficial re-uploads.

wfinfo.tierReason: Long-lived, actively maintained (49+ releases, latest v9.x), maintained by the respected WFCD group, and consistently recommended as the standard relic-reward price checker. Not tier 'essential' only because it serves the specific relic-cracking/trading audience rather than all players.

Massive time-saver — instant Plat/Ducat values on the reward screen, no alt-tabbing

Active release cadence and a Linux port (wfinfo-ng)

Requires specific setup: Borderless Fullscreen and in-game Item Labels enabled
Windows-focused (Linux users need the separate community wfinfo-ng build)
Prices are only as good as warframe.market data / connectivity
As an unofficial external overlay, safety rests on community/past-staff assurance, not a formal DE endorsement

warframe-hub.oneLiner: A free, open-source web dashboard by the Warframe Community Developers (WFCD) that displays live Warframe worldstate data — cycle timers, alerts, invasions, fissures and more.
warframe-hub.overview: Warframe Hub (hub.warframestat.us) is a browser-based companion site that parses Warframe's in-game WorldState via the WFCD Warframe Status API and presents it in a readable dashboard. It is best known for its at-a-glance cycle timers (Cetus day/night, Orb Vallis warm/cold, Cambion Drift, Zariman, Duviri) alongside alerts, invasions, void fissures, sortie/archon info, Baro Ki'Teer, Nightwave, an item codex, Riven data and synthesis targets. It is a read-only web tool maintained by volunteers as a passion project, open-sourced under Apache-2.0 and deployed on Vercel.
keyFeatures: Live cycle timers for open worlds (Cetus, Orb Vallis, Cambion Drift, Zariman, Duviri)
Worldstate tracking: alerts, invasions, void fissures, sorties/archon hunts
Baro Ki'Teer, Nightwave and other recurring-event tracking
Item codex / searchable item database
Riven mod data and synthesis target directory
Powered by the WFCD Warframe Status API (warframestat.us)
Multi-language support via Crowdin; dark theme and mobile-app view
Built with Next.js/React/TypeScript, open-source (Apache-2.0)
warframe-hub.bestFor: Players who want a quick, no-install web page to check open-world cycle timers and current worldstate events without opening the game or an app.
warframe-hub.sentimentSummary: As part of the well-regarded WFCD ecosystem, the underlying warframestat.us API is widely trusted and used by many downstream Warframe tools. Direct, quotable community discussion of the Hub front-end specifically is sparse, and there have been intermittent reports over the years of the site being down or users seeking alternatives. Overall reputation is positive and trustworthy but the front-end has a relatively modest, niche direct following compared with the API that powers it.

safety.notes: Read-only web dashboard that only consumes public worldstate data via the WFCD API. No game-memory reading, no overlay, no account login or credentials, and no real-money trading. Open-source under Apache-2.0, so it does not violate DE's rules and carries no account risk.

warframe-hub.tierReason: A polished, safe, open-source worldstate/timer dashboard from the respected WFCD team, useful to a broad range of players; not 'essential' because much of its data is also available in-game or through the many other tools built on the same API, giving it a solid-but-not-universal direct user base.

A forum thread titled 'hub.warframestat.us alternative?' indicates some users have at times looked for alternatives (e.g. during downtime), reflecting occasional reliability concerns with the front-end.

Open-source (Apache-2.0) and maintained by the trusted WFCD team
Clean at-a-glance cycle timers for all open worlds
Broad worldstate coverage (alerts, fissures, invasions, sorties, Baro, Nightwave)
Powered by the same reliable API that many other Warframe tools depend on
Multi-language support and a mobile-friendly view
cons: Historical reports of the site being down or users seeking alternatives
Read-only info dashboard — no trading, build, or account-integrated features
Much of the data overlaps with in-game screens and other WFCD-powered tools
Relatively low direct visibility/discussion compared with its underlying API
Volunteer passion project with limited guaranteed support

de-drop-tables.oneLiner: Digital Extremes' official, auto-generated drop-rate reference for Warframe's PC version — the authoritative source of truth every third-party drop tool is built on.
de-drop-tables.overview: The Official Warframe Drop Tables is a single-page HTML reference published by Digital Extremes at warframe.com/droptables (which 302-redirects to a CMS-hosted static HTML file). It is automatically generated from the game's internal data and lists drop chances for most items obtainable from missions, bounties, caches, relics, and enemies, organized by rotation (A/B/C) and rarity tier. DE explicitly disclaims completeness: the list is maintained by an automated process combined with manual publishing tied to Updates (not all Hotfixes), so newly added game systems may lag or be missing, and the data comes "with no guarantees." It is the canonical dataset that community resources like the Warframe Wiki and drops.warframestat.us parse and re-present in more searchable form.
keyFeatures: Official, first-party drop data straight from DE's internal game data
Covers mission rewards by planet, dynamic activities (Arbitrations, Void Storms, Duviri), bounty rewards, and item/mod/blueprint/resource drops
Shows per-rotation rewards (A/B/C) with rarity tiers (Common through Legendary)
Updated with major game Updates (last updated June 25, 2026 at time of research)
Free, browser-based, read-only — no account or install
Serves as the upstream data source for the Wiki, warframestat.us, and most third-party drop tools
de-drop-tables.bestFor: Players and tool developers who want the authoritative, first-party drop rate for a specific item or mission and are willing to Ctrl+F through a large raw HTML page rather than use a friendlier community front-end.
de-drop-tables.sentimentSummary: The community treats it as the trusted ground truth for drop rates — every major third-party tool derives from it — but almost nobody uses the page directly because it is one enormous, unsearchable HTML document that requires Ctrl+F. Most players prefer the Wiki or drops.warframestat.us, which parse the same official data into searchable interfaces. The main honest criticism is that DE's own disclaimer admits the data can lag behind hotfixes and may not cover brand-new systems, so it is authoritative but not always perfectly current.

safety.notes: Read-only, first-party web page hosted by Digital Extremes. No login, no download, no game-memory access, no overlay — zero account or ToS risk. It is DE's own official resource.

de-drop-tables.tierReason: It is the single authoritative, first-party source of all Warframe drop data; the entire ecosystem of drop-rate tools (Wiki, warframestat.us) is downstream of it. Foundational even though most players consume it indirectly through friendlier front-ends.

alecaframe.oneLiner: A feature-rich Overwolf companion overlay for Warframe that tracks inventory, values relic rewards, and one-click-lists items on Warframe.market.
alecaframe.overview: AlecaFrame is a PC-only third-party companion app for Warframe, distributed through the Overwolf platform. It reads your local game data (via Overwolf's inventory/log access) to show unlocked Warframes and weapons, foundry crafting trees with missing ingredients and their sources, in-game timers, relic reward values, and Riven tools. Its standout feature is deep Warframe.market integration, letting you post tradeable parts as listings with one click and see relic/set values. It is free, with some advanced market-analysis features gated behind Patreon support.
keyFeatures: Inventory manager showing unlocked Warframes, weapons, and mods
Foundry/crafting tree view highlighting missing ingredients and where to farm them
One-click Warframe.market listing creation and automatic trade closing
In-game relic overlay showing reward plat values while opening relics
Riven explorer/finder/'sniper' with stat and price comparison
Relic planner to maximize plat-per-relic profit
Set manager and trade statistics/analytics
In-game timers and drop-location lookups
alecaframe.bestFor: PC Warframe players who trade actively and want inventory tracking plus fast Warframe.market listing and relic/Riven valuation.
alecaframe.sentimentSummary: Community sentiment is broadly positive: it holds a 4.4/5 rating across 75 ratings on the Overwolf store, and multiple YouTubers call it a "must-have" for PC players, especially praising the auto-trade and relic-pricing quality-of-life. The recurring counter-current is a steady stream of "is it safe / will I get banned?" questions on the Warframe forums and YouTube, since it is an overlay that accesses account/inventory data. The developer and community generally conclude it is allowed because Overwolf handles the data access under DE's Third Party Policy, though some players remain cautious on principle.

safety.notes: Runs as an Overwolf overlay that reads your local Warframe inventory/log data and connects to your Warframe.market account (not your DE login) to post listings. It does not modify game files, inject into game memory, or automate in-game actions. The developer states data is stored locally (only anonymous analytics/optional stats leave the machine) and that account/inventory access via Overwolf is explicitly permitted under DE's Third-Party Application policy; Overwolf apps are reviewed before publication. It is not open source but is distributed unobfuscated so the code can be inspected. Rated caution rather than safe purely because it is a memory/overlay-class tool touching account data — the community repeatedly asks about ban risk, and no such tool is 100% risk-free — but there is no evidence of bans and DE's stance on Overwolf apps is permissive.

alecaframe.tierReason: Widely adopted among PC traders, strong 4.4/5 Overwolf rating, and repeatedly called a must-have by creators; held back from 'essential' by being PC/Overwolf-only, not open source, and carrying the usual overlay-tool caution.

semlar-rivencalc.oneLiner: A free web calculator that shows the possible stat ranges (buffs and curses) for any Warframe riven mod based on weapon type, disposition, and rank.
semlar-rivencalc.overview: Semlar's Riven Calculator (semlar.com/rivencalc) is a browser-based tool for Warframe players that computes the min/max stat ranges a riven mod can roll. Every riven stat has a hidden random multiplier of roughly 0.9x to 1.1x (+/-10%), and the calculator translates that variance plus the weapon's disposition, buff/curse count, and mod rank into concrete numbers. It is part of Semlar's broader suite of riven utilities, which also includes a Riven Price Guide, Riven Finder, and Riven Comparator, tools that are effectively a community standard for riven trading and valuation.
keyFeatures: Calculates possible stat ranges (min/max) for a given riven based on the +/-10% roll variance
Accounts for weapon disposition, which scales riven power per weapon
Supports selecting number of buffs (2 or 3) and presence of a curse
Filters by mod rank (Rank 0 through Rank 8) to show how stats scale when leveled
Covers different weapon/riven types (rifle, pistol, melee, etc.) with their base stat values
Free, browser-based, no account or login required
Part of an integrated Semlar riven toolset (price guide, finder, comparator)
semlar-rivencalc.bestFor: Warframe players evaluating, rolling, or pricing riven mods who want to know the best- and worst-case stat values a riven can reach before trading or investing kuva into re-rolls.
semlar-rivencalc.sentimentSummary: Semlar's tools are long-established and widely referenced within the Warframe riven-trading community, frequently cited on the Warframe forums and wiki as a go-to resource for riven stat ranges. Direct, quotable community threads were not retrievable during this research (Reddit was inaccessible to the search agent), so sentiment is inferred from the tool's persistent presence in forum/wiki discussion rather than captured verbatim. No notable criticism of the calculator's accuracy surfaced; the main community nuance discussed is the exact +/-10% roll model it uses.

safety.notes: Entirely web-based, read-only calculator. No game-memory reading, no overlay, no account login, and no real-money trading. It only performs math on values the user inputs, so it carries no Warframe ToS or account-security risk.

semlar-rivencalc.tierReason: Semlar's riven tools are a de facto standard in the Warframe riven-trading niche and are broadly used and referenced, but the rivencalc page specifically is a focused utility (stat ranges) rather than a must-have-for-everyone tool, which keeps it at recommended rather than essential.
pros: Free and instant, no signup

Handles disposition, rank, buff/curse count in one place
Backed by a well-known, long-running Warframe tool suite
No safety or ToS risk
cons: Niche purpose, only useful to players who trade or optimize rivens
Interface is utilitarian and can be unintuitive for newcomers
Relies on the community-modeled +/-10% roll formula, which some players debate
Accuracy depends on Semlar keeping disposition/base-stat data current with game updates

fandom-wiki.oneLiner: The former community Warframe encyclopedia on Fandom, now deprecated after the wiki migrated to the DE-hosted wiki.warframe.com.
fandom-wiki.overview: The WARFRAME Wiki on Fandom (warframe.fandom.com) was for years the most complete community-maintained database for Warframe, covering Warframes, weapons, mods, combat mechanics, drop data, quests, and lore. On January 31, 2025 the volunteer editor community, with the full support of Digital Extremes and backend help from Weird Gloop (the team behind the RuneScape/OSRS wikis), relocated the wiki to the official DE-hosted wiki.warframe.com. Digital Extremes no longer endorses the Fandom site as the official wiki. The Fandom version still exists but is effectively an abandoned, no-longer-maintained snapshot that grows more outdated with every game update.
keyFeatures: Comprehensive articles on Warframes, weapons, mods, Archwings, and Companions
Item stat tables, damage/status mechanics, and build breakdowns
Drop tables, relic/reward locations, and farming guides
Quest walkthroughs and extensive lore/story documentation
Community-authored per-frame and per-weapon guides
MediaWiki search, categories, and cross-linked reference pages
Free, browser-based, no account required to read
fandom-wiki.bestFor: Historically the go-to reference for any Warframe player; today only relevant as a fallback if wiki.warframe.com is unreachable, since the official site is the actively maintained successor.
fandom-wiki.sentimentSummary: The wiki content itself was universally regarded as essential and one of the best game wikis in existence. However, community sentiment toward the Fandom platform specifically was strongly negative — years of frustration over intrusive ads that take up half the screen, auto-scrolling videos, popups, and poor desktop/mobile performance, plus Fandom pushing changes the editors and even DE disliked. That accumulated frustration drove the entire editor team to migrate off Fandom to wiki.warframe.com in early 2025. The consensus now is: the knowledge is great, but use the official wiki, not the Fandom mirror.

safety.notes: Read-only web-based encyclopedia; no account, login, game-memory access, or overlay involved, so there is no account or ToS risk. The main downside is Fandom's heavy ad load and tracking, which degrade performance and privacy but are not a game-safety concern. No RMT or credential handling.

fandom-wiki.tierReason: The underlying content was 'essential' tier, but this specific Fandom instance is deprecated and no longer maintained — the entire editor community and Digital Extremes' endorsement moved to wiki.warframe.com on January 31, 2025. The Fandom page is now a stale snapshot behind heavy ads, so it should be avoided in favor of the official wiki.

wfcd-drop-data.oneLiner: A community-maintained, machine-readable JSON mirror of Warframe's official drop tables, powering many of the ecosystem's third-party tools.
wfcd-drop-data.overview: WFCD/warframe-drop-data is an open-source project by the Warframe Community Developers (WFCD) that reformats Digital Extremes' officially published drop-rate data into easy-to-parse JSON. It is sourced by scraping DE's official drop-data website (no data mining or memory reading) and is regenerated automatically when DE publishes updates. The data is exposed as static JSON files reachable over plain HTTP (usable like an API) and via an experimental searchable web UI at drops.warframestat.us. It underpins the broader WarframeStatus API and many downstream community tools, Discord bots, and websites.
keyFeatures: Complete drop tables in JSON: mission rewards (by planet/rotation), relic contents, mod/blueprint enemy drops, bounty rewards (Cetus/Solaris/Zariman), sortie rewards, syndicate inventories, and transient rewards
Single consolidated all.json plus category-specific files (blueprintLocations, relics, missionRewards, keyRewards, modLocations, etc.)
Automatically regenerated when Digital Extremes updates its official drop-data site
Accessible via plain HTTP requests, effectively a free static API with no auth or keys
Experimental searchable web UI at drops.warframestat.us plus a version-diff/compare view
Sourced from DE's official published data (no data mining, no game-memory reading)
MIT-licensed and open source; feeds the WarframeStatus API and community tools like Genesis and Warframe Hub
wfcd-drop-data.bestFor: Developers and tool-builders who need programmatic, machine-readable Warframe drop rates and item locations; also useful to players via the searchable drops.warframestat.us UI.
wfcd-drop-data.sentimentSummary: There is essentially no direct consumer review chatter (no Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, or Trustpilot pages) because this is backend infrastructure rather than an end-user app. Within the developer community, WFCD data resources are treated as the de facto trusted standard, powering the WarframeStatus API and numerous downstream bots and sites. Its reputation rests on being an accurate, auto-updated reformat of DE's own official data rather than on hype or user testimonials.

safety.notes: Read-only, web/data-only resource. It scrapes and reformats Digital Extremes' officially published drop tables (no data mining, no game-memory reading, no overlay, no account credentials or login). MIT-licensed and open source. Data carries the standard caveat that it is auto-generated and offered with no guarantees, and can lag until DE republishes after an update. No account-safety or ToS concerns.

wfcd-drop-data.tierReason: Widely relied upon as the canonical machine-readable source of Warframe drop data across the third-party ecosystem, but it is a developer/data resource rather than a mainstream player-facing app, so it lands at recommended rather than essential.

drops-warframestat.oneLiner: A searchable, developer-friendly mirror of Digital Extremes' official Warframe drop tables, maintained by the Warframe Community Developers.
drops-warframestat.overview: drops.warframestat.us is a WFCD (Warframe Community Developers) project that takes Digital Extremes' official drop-data website and republishes it in an easier-to-search, easier-to-parse format. It offers an experimental searchable web UI plus static JSON/HTTP endpoints (usable like an unofficial API) covering mission rewards, relic contents, mod and blueprint tables, bounty rewards (Cetus/Solaris/Zariman), sortie and syndicate rewards, and more. The data mirrors DE's official PC drop tables and updates when DE's source updates; a diff/compare-versions page tracks changes between builds. It is open source (MIT) on GitHub as WFCD/warframe-drop-data.
keyFeatures: Searchable web UI over the full official DE drop-table dataset
Machine-readable JSON endpoints (no auth) usable as an unofficial API
Sourced directly from DE's official drop data, not data-mined
Covers missions, relics, mods, blueprints, bounties, sorties, syndicates
Version-diff page to compare drop-table changes between builds
Auto-updates when DE's official data changes
Open source (MIT), part of the wider warframestat.us / WFCD ecosystem

It is respected as an accurate, official-sourced mirror of DE's drop tables, though the raw UI is utilitarian and most players encounter its data indirectly through polished third-party tools rather than visiting the site itself.

drops-warframestat.tierReason: A foundational, trustworthy data source that is invaluable to developers and data-minded players, but as a direct end-user tool it is bare-bones and most players consume its data through other apps rather than visiting it, so it fits a specific-need audience rather than being a broadly-adopted must-have UI.

wfcd-items.oneLiner: An open-source library that packages every Warframe item and its data directly from the game's API, published as the @wfcd/items npm package.
wfcd-items.overview: WFCD warframe-items is a data library maintained by the Warframe Community Developers (WFCD) that pulls the complete item catalogue straight from Warframe's own API rather than scraping the wiki. It ships pre-compiled JSON (plus images via the warframestat.us CDN) covering warframes, weapons, mods, relics, arcanes, resources and more, with drop rates, unique in-game paths, patch logs, riven data, tradability flags and 13-language localization. It is distributed on npm as @wfcd/items and is a foundational data source behind many community tools and bots. It is a developer-facing library, not an end-user app.
keyFeatures: Item data sourced directly from Warframe's official API (no wiki scraping)
23+ categories: Warframes, Primary/Secondary/Melee, Mods, Relics, Arcanes, Resources, Pets, etc.
Pre-compiled JSON data plus per-item drop rates and patch logs
Unique in-game item paths (e.g. /Lotus/Weapons/...) and tradability status
Riven weapon data included
Images served via cdn.warframestat.us CDN (too large for npm)
CommonJS and ESM support with configurable category loading
Internationalization across 13 languages
TypeScript type definitions and a findItem() lookup utility
MIT licensed, auto-updated with each game release (5,000+ releases published)
wfcd-items.bestFor: Developers building Warframe community tools, bots, or sites who need a complete, regularly-updated, structured item dataset without scraping the wiki.
wfcd-items.sentimentSummary: There is little public consumer-style discussion because this is a developer library, not a player-facing app. Its reputation rests on real adoption: it is an official WFCD project that underpins the broader warframestat.us API and numerous community tools (e.g. Genesis, Warframe Hub). GitHub metrics (397 stars, 72 forks, only 6 open issues) and steady npm usage (~2.4k weekly / ~13.8k monthly downloads) indicate a trusted, actively maintained data source within the Warframe developer community.

safety.notes: Read-only open-source (MIT) data library that only fetches/packages publicly available item data from Warframe's API. It does not read game memory, does not overlay the game, and requires no account credentials or login, so it poses no ToS or account-security risk to players. Not related to RMT.

wfcd-items.tierReason: Within its audience (developers) it is a de-facto standard, widely depended-upon data source and an official WFCD project that is actively maintained and updated daily. It is not tier 'essential' only because it serves developers rather than general players, and 'niche' understates its broad adoption as backbone data for many community tools.

warframe-companion.oneLiner: Digital Extremes' official free mobile app that lets you manage your Foundry, extractors, and account info from your phone while away from the game.
warframe-companion.overview: Warframe Companion is the official first-party mobile app for Warframe, developed and published by Digital Extremes for Android and iOS (originally launched in 2013 as "Warframe Nexus"). Without logging in it shows live worldstate data such as news, Nightwave challenges, Invasions, Sorties, and Void Fissures across all platforms, plus a Codex. Logging in with your PC, PlayStation, Xbox, or Nintendo account unlocks account-linked actions: deploying and collecting extractor drones, starting and claiming Foundry builds, managing Void Relics, viewing Arsenal/Inventory, interacting with companions, and clan/friend chat. It is a genuine first-party account app, not a third-party overlay or scraper.
keyFeatures: Start and claim Foundry builds (Warframes, weapons, gear) remotely while offline
Deploy and collect extractor drones to farm resources passively
View live worldstate: news, Nightwave, Invasions, Sorties, Void Fissures across all platforms
Manage and upgrade Void Relics
View Arsenal and Inventory
Clan and friend chat plus syndicate standing
Codex access without login
Official account login via PC/PSN/Xbox/Nintendo
warframe-companion.bestFor: Existing Warframe players who want to keep their Foundry and extractor drones productive while away from their PC or console, and check live world state on the go.
warframe-companion.sentimentSummary: Sentiment is polarized by platform: the iOS version is highly rated (4.8/5 from ~15,000 ratings) with users calling it "borderline essential," while the Android/Google Play version sits lower at about 3.8/5 despite 1M+ downloads. Common praise is for remote Foundry claiming and extractor management; common complaints are having to log in every session, slow Foundry pages, occasional crashes, missing in-game features (no loadout changes, limited clan tools), and reliability outages. The app was temporarily delisted from the iOS App Store before being relisted on April 15, 2024.

Free on both Android and iOS
Foundry and extractor management genuinely useful when away from the game

de-public-export.oneLiner: Digital Extremes' official public data endpoint that exports Warframe's game data (weapons, warframes, mods, relics, drops) as localized JSON for third-party tools.
de-public-export.overview: Warframe Public Export (formerly "Mobile Export") is a read-only, unauthenticated data endpoint operated by Digital Extremes that distributes the game's internal data to the official Warframe Companion mobile app, the Arsenal Twitch Extension, and the game client itself. Clients first fetch an LZMA-compressed, language-specific index from origin.warframe.com, which lists content-hashed manifest filenames; the actual JSON manifests (weapons, warframes, companions, mods/rivens, blueprints, resources, relics, arcanes, star-chart nodes, cosmetics) and image assets are then served from content.warframe.com's CDN. Because each file name ends in a 26-character content hash that changes on every update, clients can cache assets permanently but must re-poll the index to detect changes. It supports all of Warframe's officially supported languages, localizing names, descriptions, and level stats. It is the authoritative upstream source that most Warframe third-party data tools and APIs ultimately derive from.
keyFeatures: Official first-party data source maintained by Digital Extremes (highest authority/accuracy)
Read-only and unauthenticated - no API key, no account, no login required
Content-addressed hashing: filenames carry a 26-char hash so assets are permanently cacheable via DE's CDN
Two-step access: LZMA-compressed index at origin.warframe.com, then JSON manifests at content.warframe.com/PublicExport/Manifest/
Broad coverage: weapons, warframes, companions, mods/rivens, blueprints, resources, relics, arcanes, star-chart nodes, cosmetics
Full localization across all officially supported Warframe languages
ExportManifest.json maps each item's uniqueName to a textureLocation for retrieving image assets
de-public-export.bestFor: Developers and data maintainers building Warframe third-party tools, apps, or wikis who need ground-truth game data straight from the source; not a tool players interact with directly.
de-public-export.sentimentSummary: Among Warframe developers, Public Export is respected as the authoritative source of truth and is the upstream that community APIs (warframestat.us, WFCD, warframe-public-export cleanups) are built on. However, the raw format draws consistent criticism for being awkward to consume: the LZMA index, frequently changing content hashes, malformed/non-standard JSON needing correction, sparse documentation, and gaps in coverage. Those gaps are precisely why community mirrors and enhanced projects like calamity-inc's warframe-public-export (cleaned JSON) and warframe-public-export-plus ("everything missing in Public Export and more") exist and see steady use.

safety.notes: Read-only, unauthenticated, first-party HTTP data endpoint hosted by Digital Extremes. No game-memory reading, no overlay, no account credentials, and no real-money trading involved - it is the official data feed DE itself uses for the Companion app and Twitch extension, so it carries no ToS or account risk. The only practical caution is technical: consumers should poll the index and handle the frequently changing content hashes and non-standard JSON rather than hardcoding URLs.

de-public-export.tierReason: It is the official, authoritative upstream data source that effectively every Warframe data tool depends on, which makes it foundational for developers. It falls short of 'essential' as a listed tool because it is developer-only (not player-facing) and its raw form is painful enough that most builders consume it via cleaned community wrappers/mirrors rather than directly.

worldstate-parser.oneLiner: An open-source JavaScript/TypeScript library from the Warframe Community Developers that parses Warframe's raw worldstate feed into clean, usable objects.
worldstate-parser.overview: warframe-worldstate-parser is a Node.js/TypeScript library maintained by WFCD (Warframe Community Developers) that converts Warframe's raw worldState.php JSON into structured, developer-friendly JavaScript objects for events like alerts, invasions, fissures, sorties, Baro Ki'Teer, Cetus/Fortuna cycles, and more. It is the parsing engine behind the wider WFCD ecosystem, including the warframe-status API (docs.warframestat.us), the Genesis Discord bot, and Warframe Hub. It is not an end-user app but a building block that powers a large share of community-made Warframe tools, bots, and websites.

Adoption signals (GitHub presence, long release history, downstream dependents) point to steady trust; most end users benefit from it indirectly without knowing it exists.

public-export-plus.oneLiner: An open-source, enhanced JSON data dump of Warframe's game data that fills the gaps in Digital Extremes' official Public Export.
public-export-plus.overview: warframe-public-export-plus is a community-maintained data source (by calamity-inc) that builds on Warframe's official "Public Export" — the endpoint DE uses to feed internal game data to its Companion app and Arsenal Twitch extension. It reorganizes and extends that data into 50+ clean JSON files (Export*.json) with localization split out separately (dict.*.json across ~13 languages), covering warframes, weapons, mods, relics, arcanes, recipes, missions, vendors, and more. It is distributed via GitHub and as an npm package, and powers the open-source browse.wf data browser. It is a developer/data resource, not an in-game tool or app.

browse-wf.oneLiner: An open-source web tool for exploring raw Warframe game data, tracking the live world state, and viewing profiles/inventory.
browse-wf.overview: browse.wf is an open-source website by calamity-inc (the team behind AlecaFrame) that resolves Warframe's internal data paths and returns the underlying game data for any queried object, describing itself as "like a search engine, but for space ninjas." Beyond raw data exploration it bundles a live world-state tracker (bounties, invasions, arbitration schedule, Deep Archimedea modifiers), a Riven calculator, Prime Vault tracker, KIM/Kimulacrum conversation tools, a profile viewer, and an optional PC inventory sync. The site is localized into 15 languages and is a favorite among theorycrafters, data miners, and content creators who need authoritative game data.
keyFeatures: Raw game-data explorer that resolves Warframe internal paths and returns JSON/data for any item, frame, weapon, or image
Live World State tracker: current bounties, invasions, arbitration schedule, Deep Archimedea modifiers
Riven Calculator for analyzing weapon riven mod rolls
Prime Vault tracker and Invigorations reference
Profile Viewer (now requires an account id since update 38.0.8, not username)
Optional PC inventory sync via lastData.dat (from AlecaFrame or warframe-api-helper) with data kept client-side in the browser

Localized into 15 languages
browse-wf.bestFor: Theorycrafters, data miners, wiki editors, and advanced players who need to inspect authoritative raw Warframe data, track the live world state, or evaluate rivens.
browse-wf.sentimentSummary: browse.wf is quietly well-regarded within the Warframe data/theorycrafting community and is maintained by a reputable team (calamity-inc / AlecaFrame), but it has a small, specialized audience rather than mass adoption. External review evidence is thin: no dedicated Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, or Trustpilot page surfaced in research, which is typical for a niche data-exploration utility rather than a sign of negative reception.

safety.notes: Primarily a read-only web tool; explored data stays client-side. The optional inventory sync never asks for account passwords or credentials — it reads a local lastData.dat file produced by AlecaFrame or the warframe-api-helper (PC only) and the page states the data stays in your browser. Profile viewing uses DE's own public API/account id. The only mild consideration is that the inventory feature ties into AlecaFrame (a third-party companion tool that reads game network data); browse.wf itself is a website and does not read game memory or inject into the client.

browse-wf.tierReason: Extremely useful for a specific audience (data exploration, live world state, riven math) and backed by a trusted dev team, but it is a specialist/data-nerd utility with a modest footprint (~40 GitHub stars, no mainstream review coverage) rather than a broadly essential daily tool.

quantframe.oneLiner: A free, open-source desktop app that algorithmically automates buy/sell listings on Warframe.market and tracks your trading profit.
quantframe.overview: Quantframe is a quantitative/algorithmic trading application for Warframe.market. It connects to your Warframe.market account via the site's API and automatically creates and updates buy and sell orders based on real-time market data, while tracking your inventory, trade history, profit and volume in a local SQLite database. It is a Tauri-based desktop app (Rust backend, React/TypeScript frontend) for Windows and Linux, inspired by akmayer's Warframe-Algo-Trader. The core app is open source (GPL-3.0), with an optional paid Patreon tier for supporters/extra access.
keyFeatures: Automatic creation and updating of buy/sell orders on Warframe.market based on market data
Inventory management: set minimum sell prices, mark items as sold
Trade history dashboard with profit and volume statistics
Riven mod management for listing rivens for sale

Live-trading settings/strategy configuration
quantframe.bestFor: Dedicated Warframe.market traders and plat flippers who want to automate high-volume buy-low/sell-high listing management rather than doing it manually.
quantframe.sentimentSummary: Concrete community discussion is thin: no genuine Reddit threads surfaced in searches, and coverage is mostly from a handful of YouTube "market flipping / auto-trader" videos plus a dedicated Patreon, framing it as a way to earn passive plat. The project itself shows healthy adoption for a niche tool (146 stars on the main repo, 44 releases, actively maintained into mid-2026). Sentiment where present is cautiously positive around convenience, though some creator videos openly show mixed/negative short-term results ("Big Loss or Future Profit?", "I Lost 186 Platinum?!"), reflecting that automated flipping does not guarantee profit.

safety.notes: Quantframe does NOT read game memory or inject into the Warframe client, so DE's stance on overlays/memory-readers is not the relevant risk axis. Instead it logs into your Warframe.market account (a community-run third-party site, not operated by DE) and automates order posting via that site's API. Automated/bot-style activity and credential/session use put your Warframe.market account (not your game account) at potential risk if it conflicts with Warframe.market's rules or rate limits. The app is open source (GPL-3.0), so the code is auditable, which mitigates malware concern; a third-party URL scanner rated quantframe.app a middling ~67/100 trust score. Use with awareness of Warframe.market's automation policies.

quantframe.tierReason: Actively developed, open source, and genuinely useful for serious market flippers, but automated trading is a specialized use case that most players do not need, and it carries Warframe.market account-risk. Adoption is solid-but-niche (low-hundreds of GitHub stars), not broadly essential.

underframe.oneLiner: A free web-based Warframe build creator and deep DPS/damage-simulation calculator with an auto-build optimizer and Riven tooling.
underframe.overview: Underframe (underframe.site) is a browser-based build and theorycrafting tool for Warframe. It lets players assemble builds for Warframes, weapons and companions, then runs a combat/DPS simulation against customizable target dummies (faction, level scaling, headshot/weakpoint multipliers, status effects) rather than relying on simple stat totals. Beyond manual building it offers an auto-build optimizer with tunable scoring, Riven creation and auto-rolling, an AoE layout editor, mod import from images, and shareable snapshot links. It is free, requires no account, and stores data locally in the browser; feedback and build submissions are handled through a Discord community.

Partner/buff linking so a linked Warframe's buffs apply to weapon builds
Build sharing via snapshot links (with link minification) and import/export
Curated featured/expert-vetted builds (e.g. Mag, Mirage, Wisp)
underframe.bestFor: Theorycrafters and min-maxers who want a deeper, simulation-based DPS calculator and auto-optimizer than a basic build sharer offers.
underframe.sentimentSummary: There is very little public third-party discussion of Underframe. The tool itself is real, functional and notably feature-deep (its combat simulation and auto-build go beyond simple stat-sheet builders like Overframe), but it has a small footprint compared with established sites. No Reddit threads, store ratings, or review pages surfaced; the only external content found was a single creator YouTube showcase, with community activity centered on its Discord.

safety.notes: Web-based, read-only theorycrafting tool. No game login, no account required, no memory reading or overlay, and no RMT. Build data is stored locally in the browser and a privacy policy is provided. No DE ToS concerns.

underframe.tierReason: A legitimately capable, simulation-driven build/DPS calculator, but with minimal visible community adoption or third-party coverage versus dominant sites like Overframe and Reframed. Strong for dedicated theorycrafters; small audience overall.

tenno-tools.oneLiner: A clean, no-login web dashboard for tracking Warframe's live worldstate (alerts, invasions, sorties) plus a syndicate-standing calculator.
tenno-tools.overview: Tenno Tools (tenno.tools) is an unofficial, fan-made web application that acts as a Warframe "World Tracker," giving a quick snapshot of the Origin System without needing to log into the game. Its status page tracks ongoing activities such as alerts, invasions and sorties across PC, PlayStation, Xbox and Switch, and it also includes a Syndicate Simulator/Calculator that lets players plan how gaining standing with one syndicate affects opposing ones before spending in-game reputation. It was built by a developer known as "inorganicorganist" on Reddit, with source (at least the syndicate planner) published open-source under MIT on GitHub. Digital Extremes lists it in their official "Warframe Tools and Resources Every Player Should Know About" article.

It appears well-regarded but niche, overshadowed in mindshare by larger worldstate resources.

tenno-tracker.oneLiner: A web-based, cross-platform Warframe mastery tracker with profile sync, unmastered-item roadmaps, and optional farming/market tools.
tenno-tracker.overview: Tenno Tracker (tennotracker.com) is a browser-based companion tool that analyzes a player's Warframe profile to show which items remain unmastered, organized by category, and lets users build roadmaps with crafting-requirement and drop-location breakdowns. It works across PC, PlayStation, Xbox, Switch and mobile via both manual and automatic profile sync, pulling data from the official Digital Extremes API plus community resources rather than reading game memory or hooking the client. It runs a freemium model with paid Supporter and Ally tiers that add faster sync, more roadmaps, optimized multi-item farming routes, and live platinum market prices. The project is independent (explicitly "not affiliated with Digital Extremes") and is funded through Patreon.
keyFeatures: Profile analysis showing unmastered items grouped by category with where to obtain them

Component drop-location data built into the item database
Optimized farming routes / multi-item mission recommendations (paid tier)
Real-time platinum market price integration for tradable items (paid tier)
No installs, no client hooks, no game-memory reading — uses the official DE API
tenno-tracker.bestFor: Cross-platform or console Warframe players who want a no-install web tool to see exactly what they still need to master and plan farming/crafting roadmaps toward Mastery Rank.
tenno-tracker.sentimentSummary: Independent community feedback is currently sparse: there is a Warframe Forums Fan Zone thread announcing the tool and a modest but real support base (about 230 Patreon members; the companion Firefox GID-fetcher extension has roughly 174 users and no ratings yet). No Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, or Trustpilot pages discussing it were found, so there is little independent sentiment to synthesize. The tool positions itself around safety and cross-platform coverage, and its documented approach (official DE API, no memory reading) is consistent with that claim, but broad adoption appears small and it competes with more established mastery trackers.

safety.notes: Web-based and read-only: it pulls profile data through the official Digital Extremes API and community resources, with no game-memory reading and no client hooks, which is the category of tool DE permits. Auto-sync relies on an optional Firefox 'GID Fetcher' extension (MIT-licensed, ~174 users, optional access scoped to warframe.com) that retrieves your Warframe GID session cookie to link your account; this is a session-cookie/account-linking mechanism, so users should install only the official extension, though it is not a game-credential harvester or overlay. No RMT. It is explicitly not affiliated with Digital Extremes. Paid tiers exist but are optional supporter subscriptions.

cephalon-navis.oneLiner: A free open-source Warframe companion app for Android and iOS that surfaces live worldstate data (fissures, cycles, Baro, sorties, Nightwave) from the WarframeStat.us API.
cephalon-navis.overview: Cephalon Navis is a mobile Warframe "hub" app maintained by WFCD (Warframe Community Developers), the same community group behind the WarframeStat.us API and other well-known fan tools. It pulls public worldstate data and presents in-game news, world events, open-world day/night cycle timers, bounties, void fissures, Nightwave challenges, Darvo's daily deals, Baro Ki'Teer timer and inventory, sorties, Archon hunts, and invasions, with platform-specific worldstates (PC/PS/Xbox/Switch). It is read-only and does not connect to a player's Warframe account. It is not affiliated with Digital Extremes.

genesis.oneLiner: Cephalon Genesis is WFCD's open-source Warframe Discord bot for tracking worldstate events, item lookups, and market prices across your server.
genesis.overview: Genesis (Cephalon Genesis) is a free, open-source Discord bot maintained by Warframe Community Developers (WFCD), the same group behind warframestat.us and the Warframe Hub. It lets Discord servers track and get notified about Warframe worldstate events (alerts, invasions, fissures, sorties, Baro Ki'Teer, cycles, etc.), look up items and drop data, check Warframe.market prices, and manage LFG, giveaways, custom commands, and dynamic voice channels. Configuration is done via slash commands in Discord plus a web dashboard at genesis.warframestat.us. It runs on roughly 26,000 servers.

Web dashboard at genesis.warframestat.us for per-server settings
Multi-language support (Crowdin) and Twitch stream monitoring for the Warframe channel
genesis.bestFor: Warframe Discord server owners and clan/community admins who want automated in-server event tracking, notifications, and item/market lookups.
genesis.sentimentSummary: Direct community discussion is sparse but positive where it exists. Genesis is a long-standing, trusted WFCD project (the same team behind the widely used warframestat.us API), deployed on ~26,000 servers, which signals broad real adoption. Its top.gg listing shows a 4.25/5 average from a small number of reviews, with users calling it "really good" while wishing for more granular permission and auto-update options. No significant criticism or safety complaints surfaced.

safety.notes: Web/Discord-based bot that reads public Warframe worldstate and market data; it does not read game memory, use overlays, or require Warframe/DE account credentials, so it does not conflict with DE's stance on third-party tools. Open-source under Apache 2.0 by the reputable WFCD group. Standard Discord bot permission caution applies (grant only needed server permissions), but no RMT or account-risk concerns.

genesis.tierReason: Widely adopted (~26k servers), maintained by the trusted WFCD team, actively developed (v3.1.1 released July 2026), and free/open-source. It is the de facto Warframe Discord bot, though it serves the specific need of Discord communities rather than every individual player, so 'recommended' rather than 'essential'.

rivenradar.oneLiner: A web-based Warframe riven price analyzer and deal-finder that ranks live warframe.market auctions by how underpriced they are.
rivenradar.overview: RivenRadar is an independent fan-made web tool that values Warframe riven mods and surfaces underpriced listings on warframe.market. You can paste an auction URL or manually describe a roll to get a typical asking-price range plus a confidence rating, and a live "deal feed" ranks current listings by margin. It uses a comparable-bucket pricing model (matching weapon, positive-stat combination, and negative-stat category, anchored on the 15th-percentile price with a 25th-75th-percentile asking range). It is not affiliated with warframe.market or Digital Extremes.
keyFeatures: Riven Analyzer: paste a warframe.market auction or describe a roll to get typical asking price, spread, and confidence
Deal Feed: live underpriced listings ranked by margin and confidence with visible bucket math
Market Pulse: daily riven price tracking per weapon across 24h/7d/14d/30d, showing climbers, dips, and listing surges
Watchlist rules: saved filters (weapon, max price, min discount, required stats, online-only sellers) that filter the deal feed
Comparable-bucket valuation model using 15th-percentile anchor and 25th-75th-percentile range
Premium extras: comparable listings, suggested counter-offer, distribution chart, per-part pricing, arbitrage leaderboard
No game account or login required to use core features
rivenradar.bestFor: Riven traders who want to check what a roll is worth and hunt for underpriced auctions on warframe.market without manually cross-referencing listings.
rivenradar.sentimentSummary: No external community discussion of RivenRadar could be found — no Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, Trustpilot page, Product Hunt listing, or public GitHub repo surfaced in searches, with all results pointing back to the tool's own site. This suggests a new or very low-profile tool, so there is no independent sentiment (positive or negative) to report yet. Its stated valuation methodology is transparent and reasonable, but adoption and accuracy remain unverified by third parties.

safety.notes: Read-only web app that works in the browser with no game account, login, or credentials required and no client/overlay that reads game memory, so it poses no account or ToS risk. It relies on public warframe.market data. Note only that it is an independent fan project (not affiliated with warframe.market or DE) and charges a one-time $14.99 for its optional Premium tier.

rivenradar.tierReason: Functionally solid and safe, but no evidence of community adoption or third-party reviews was found, and it competes in a space with established free riven pricing tools. Placed as niche pending real usage signals rather than recommended.
pros: Transparent, documented valuation methodology (comparable-bucket, percentile-based)
Core analyzer, deal feed, and market trends are free and require no account or login
One-time $14.99 premium with no subscription or recurring charges
Deal feed and market-pulse trend tracking go beyond a simple price lookup
cons: No independent reviews, Reddit discussion, or community footprint found to verify accuracy or reliability
Depends entirely on warframe.market listing data, inheriting its gaps and stale/troll listings
Some of the most useful features (comparables, distribution chart, per-part pricing) are paywalled behind Premium
Riven valuation is inherently uncertain, so automated estimates should be treated as guidance, not gospel

warframe-reliquary.oneLiner: A free web-based prime wishlist, relic-planning, and fissure-tracking tool that maps your wanted Prime parts to the relics and mission locations that yield them.
warframe-reliquary.overview: Warframe Reliquary (wf.xuerian.net) is a fan-made, browser-based farming planner built by Xuerian. You wishlist the Prime items you want, and the tool shows which relics contain those parts, which drop tables/starmap locations reward those relics, and which of those locations currently have an active fissure (highlighted in gold). Data is sourced from Digital Extremes' officially published drop tables, and vaulted items are flagged in red. Wishlists can optionally be saved, generating a private bookmark key and a shareable public key so friends can view each other's lists.
keyFeatures: Prime part wishlist that filters down to only the relics you need
Reliquary view: relic-to-item mapping plus which drop tables contain each relic
Live fissure tracking — starmap location groups with an active fissure are highlighted in gold
Vaulted items flagged in red so you avoid unobtainable targets
Saveable wishlists with a private bookmark key and shareable public key for co-planning with friends
Direct deep-linking to specific primes/parts via URL parameters (e.g. ?parts=rhino,nyx:chassis)
Uses official Digital Extremes drop-table data
Multi-language locale support (enUS, ptBR, frFR, etc.)
warframe-reliquary.bestFor: Players actively farming Prime parts who want to plan efficient relic runs and know which fissures to run right now, especially for coordinated squad/friend farming.
warframe-reliquary.sentimentSummary: Direct community discussion is sparse — there is an official Warframe Forums announcement/support thread and it is cited in a Steam community guide and forum "good relic planners" discussions as one of the few relic planners kept current with DE drop data. No Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, or store/Trustpilot ratings were found. The available references are neutral-to-positive, framing it as a reliable, up-to-date planning utility, with the main caveat being that many older competing relic planners have gone stale.

safety.notes: Web-based, read-only planning tool. It does not read game memory, overlay the client, or require Warframe/DE account credentials — it only uses publicly published drop-table data, so it falls outside DE's overlay/memory-reading concerns and carries no account or RMT risk. Optional saved wishlists use generated keys rather than logins.

warframe-reliquary.tierReason: A capable, up-to-date relic planner recommended in guides as one of the few still maintained, but it serves a specific need (Prime farm planning) and has a small, quiet community footprint rather than broad must-have adoption.

otherReviews.snippet: Official fan-zone thread titled 'Warframe Reliquary wishlist and relic planner' where the tool is presented/supported by its creator (thread returned HTTP 403 to automated fetch but is indexed and publicly listed).

warframe-appraiser.oneLiner: A free, browser-based tool that appraises your Warframe inventory from a screenshot using OCR and live warframe.market platinum prices.
warframe-appraiser.overview: Warframe Appraiser is a free, mobile-friendly web app that lets players photograph or upload a screenshot of their in-game inventory and instantly get platinum valuations pulled live from warframe.market. Basic scanning works without an account, while signing in unlocks saved inventory ("My Stash") with quantities, variants and market snapshots, item state management (Keep/Listed/Sold/Ignored), set-completion analysis, relic valuation, and a manual mastery tracker. It also includes a separate Riven Appraiser for evaluating unveiled riven rolls. The tool is community-made, is not affiliated with Digital Extremes, and is funded by donations ("Buy me a coffee").
keyFeatures: OCR scanning of inventory screenshots/photos (no login required for basic use)
Live platinum pricing sourced from warframe.market
My Stash: saved inventory with quantities, variants, and market snapshots (requires sign-in)
Item state management: Keep, Listed, Sold, Ignored
Set-completion analysis and warframe.market sell-order references
Relic valuation
Manual mastery/equipment rank and mastered-state tracker
Companion Riven Appraiser for unveiled riven rolls
Mobile-friendly, browser-based (no install)
warframe-appraiser.bestFor: Players who want a quick, no-install way to estimate the platinum value of their whole inventory or specific items from a screenshot, especially on mobile, and to track a stash over time.
warframe-appraiser.sentimentSummary: External community evidence is very thin: the tool appears new and niche, promoted mainly through the creator's own Warframe Forums announcement threads. No Reddit discussions, dedicated YouTube reviews, or Trustpilot pages were found. The creator openly notes the lightweight OCR is not always accurate and that results should be double-checked, and that the tool is English-only for both interface and scanning.

safety.notes: Read-only web tool: it processes an uploaded screenshot and reads public warframe.market prices. It does not read game memory, inject overlays, or require Warframe/DE account credentials, so it does not run afoul of DE's stance on tools that read game memory. Optional sign-in is for saving a stash only. Not affiliated with Digital Extremes. No RMT involvement. Main caveat is accuracy, not safety: the creator warns OCR can misidentify items, so verify before acting.

warframe-appraiser.tierReason: Genuinely useful concept (screenshot-to-platinum appraisal plus stash tracking), but it is a new tool with little visible adoption or independent community feedback, English-only, and reliant on imperfect OCR. That places it as a niche pick rather than a widely-adopted staple.

morrowshore-riven.oneLiner: A free, browser-based tool that reads a Riven mod screenshot with local OCR, rates each stat against the current meta, and estimates its platinum value.
morrowshore-riven.overview: The Warframe Riven Appraiser by Morrow Shore is a free web tool for evaluating unveiled Riven mods. You supply a Riven image (file upload, clipboard paste, drag-and-drop, or camera capture) and it auto-corrects rotation/skew, uses OCR to extract the weapon name and stats, color-codes each stat by its meta value, and produces a quality rating plus an estimated platinum price based on market data. OCR runs locally in the browser so mod data is not sent to a server, and it supports both single-Riven and side-by-side comparison images. It sits alongside Morrow Shore's broader set of Warframe guides (including a comprehensive Riven guide).
keyFeatures: OCR extraction of weapon name and stats from a Riven screenshot
Multiple input methods: file select, clipboard paste (Ctrl+V), drag-and-drop, and camera capture
Automatic rotation/skew correction on the input image
Per-stat color-coding and quality rating based on the current community meta
Estimated platinum value based on market data
Single-Riven and side-by-side comparison modes
Manual editing: click to correct weapon/stats, add or remove stats, plus a shorthand manual-input option
OCR runs locally in the browser (data not handled server-side) for privacy
morrowshore-riven.bestFor: Riven traders and players who want a quick screenshot-based sanity check on an unveiled Riven's stat quality and rough platinum value without manually typing rolls into a calculator.
morrowshore-riven.sentimentSummary: Direct community discussion of this specific tool is sparse in the sources I could reach, so sentiment is hard to gauge from external chatter. Morrow Shore is a recognized name for Warframe guides, and the tool's described feature set (local-OCR privacy, meta color-coding, plat estimate) is well aligned with what Riven traders ask for. The main caveat comes from the developer itself: lightweight browser OCR is not always accurate, so detected stats and weapon names should be verified before acting on the output.

safety.notes: Web-based, read-only tool. It does not read game memory, does not run as an overlay, and requires no Warframe/third-party login. OCR is described as running locally in the browser, so uploaded Riven images are not handled server-side. No RMT or account-credential exposure. No DE ToS concern.

morrowshore-riven.tierReason: Genuinely useful for the specific task of pricing/rating an unveiled Riven from a screenshot, but it serves a narrow audience (Riven traders) and competes with established calculators (Semlar rivencalc, browse.wf). Limited external evidence of broad adoption, and the developer-acknowledged OCR inaccuracy means it is a convenience aid rather than an authoritative pricer.

altair.oneLiner: Altair is a feature-rich Warframe Discord bot for riven grading, price checking, and world-state alerts, running on roughly 16,000 servers.
altair.overview: Altair (hosted at empx.cc, made by developer "empdarkness") is an expansive Warframe-focused Discord bot. It provides world-state alerts, riven stat-roll checking and grading, Warframe Market price/auction lookups, detailed weapon data (quantization, IPS rolls, incarnon stats), Baro Ki'teer inventory tracking, sortie/archon/arbitration info, and in-game profile lookups. It operates through Discord slash commands and is one of the more widely adopted Warframe utility bots, listed on top.gg and Discord Bot List with roughly 16,200 servers.

Riven stat-roll checking and manual riven grading with good/bad indicators
Warframe Market price checking for items and rivens, plus WFM auction browsing/notifications

Sortie, Archon, and arbitration status

Loadout grading

altair.sentimentSummary: Direct community discussion evidence is thin — no Reddit threads or YouTube reviews surfaced in searches — but adoption metrics are strong, with about 16,200 Discord servers using the bot per Discord Bot List. Its top.gg listing shows a 5/5 rating, though from only 3 reviews, so the star rating is not statistically meaningful. Overall it reads as a genuinely useful, actively maintained Warframe utility bot rather than a hyped one.

safety.notes: Read-only Discord bot that pulls from public Warframe/Warframe Market data via Discord slash commands. It does not read game memory, overlay the client, or require Warframe account credentials, so it carries no DE ToS or account-ban risk. Adding it only grants standard Discord bot permissions in a server.

altair.tierReason: Broad real adoption (~16,200 Discord servers) and a comprehensive, well-maintained riven/price/world-state feature set make it a strong pick for Warframe Discord servers; not 'essential' since it is Discord-bound and overlaps with standalone web tools, and direct sentiment evidence (Reddit/YouTube) was scarce.

samodeus.oneLiner: A Warframe Discord bot that grades riven mods from screenshots via OCR and calculates weapon DPS.
samodeus.overview: Samodeus (also listed as TheroxBot), by developer Theroxenes, is a Warframe companion Discord bot built with discord.py. Its headline feature is OCR-based riven mod analysis: users upload an in-game screenshot and the bot reads the stats and grades each one by variance from the stat average. It also does weapon DPS calculations for custom builds, shows riven dispositions, and provides configurable newsfeeds for Warframe updates and forum posts. The bot uses a customizable "$" prefix and is listed on top.gg with roughly 7,500 servers.

samodeus.bestFor: Warframe players and trading/clan Discord servers who want quick riven mod grading and DPS math without leaving Discord.
samodeus.sentimentSummary: External community discussion of Samodeus is very thin. Its ~7,500-server footprint suggests real, if quiet, adoption within Warframe Discord communities, but no substantial Reddit threads, YouTube reviews, or written reviews were found to gauge broader sentiment.

safety.notes: Read-only Discord bot: it processes screenshots users voluntarily upload and returns text analysis. It does not read game memory, does not overlay the game client, and requires no Warframe account credentials or login, so it raises none of DE's concerns about memory-reading overlays or account-risk tools. Standard Discord bot permissions apply.

samodeus.tierReason: Solves a specific need (riven grading and DPS inside Discord) and has a nontrivial ~7,500-server install base, but external evidence of active use or community endorsement is minimal, keeping it below 'recommended.'
